articleTitle: The Night I Realized I'd Been Teaching My Kid to Earn Love

pinterest.title: The Night I Realized I'd Accidentally Taught My Kid That Love Is Earned
pinterest.description: She asked "Are you proud of me?" after eating her pasta. And I realized — the way she asked it — she was checking. Running the numbers. Wondering if she'd measured up. I'm the one who taught her to do that. Here's what I changed, and the one sentence I wish I'd started saying sooner.

pinterest.title: "Are you proud of me?" — the question behind the question.
pinterest.description: When a 4-year-old asks "are you proud of me?" after dinner, they're not really asking about dinner. They're asking: Am I okay? Am I enough? Am I still loved even if tomorrow goes worse? Here's how to answer — and how to change what they expect to hear.

pinterest.title: By Age 4, Many Children Are Already Checking to See If They Measured Up
pinterest.description: It happens incrementally. Every time we praise behavior, we're teaching children that love responds to performance. By preschool age, many kids have learned to watch for cues — to check: was that enough? Here's the quiet shift that changes the pattern.

pinterest.title: Does Your Child Brace a Little Before Asking If You're Proud?
pinterest.description: That small brace — the pause before asking, the hopeful worried look — is a child who has learned that the answer might change. It's also entirely fixable. One sentence at bedtime, repeated over time, changes what they expect to hear.

pinterest.title: 3 Sentences to Say Tonight That Don't Depend on How the Day Went
pinterest.description: 1. 'You are loved — not because of today. Just because you're you.' 2. 'You don't have to be good tomorrow to deserve love. You already have it.' 3. 'You're my person. That doesn't change.' Say one of these tonight. Mean it. Repeat it. This is where identity is built.
keywords: what to say kids at bedtime

instagram.caption:
She was four years old. She'd just eaten most of her pasta.

She looked up at me while I was clearing plates and asked, very seriously:

"Mama, are you proud of me?"

And I said yes, of course. Because I was. Because she'd eaten her pasta.

But the way she asked it stayed with me.

Not the delighted way kids ask when they've done something spectacular. More like... checking. Like she needed to see if the numbers added up.

*Am I okay? Did I do enough today?*

And I sat there later with cold tea and realized: I taught her to ask that.

Not on purpose. Just incrementally. Every "good job" after every meal. Every "I'm so proud of you" after every achievement. All of it loving. All of it, over time, teaching a specific thing:

*Love is a response to performance.*

I didn't stop praising her. That's not the point.

I just started separating the two things that had gotten tangled:

Praise for behavior. And declarations of identity.

And every night, I started adding:

*"You are already loved. Not because of today. Just because you're you."*

She still asks "are you proud of me?" sometimes. But she asks it differently now. More like curiosity than calculation.

More like she already knows the answer.

That's what we're going for.

💛 Full story at link in bio.

#motherhood #raisingkids #unconditionallovekids #toddlermom #identityfirstparenting #alreadyloved #parentinghonestly #momlife #consciousparenting

linkedin.post:
A small moment that changed how I parent:

My four-year-old asked "are you proud of me?" after eating dinner.

Not excited. Not delighted. Just... checking.

And I realized: by consistently praising her behavior, I'd taught her that love was a response to performance. Not intentionally. Incrementally. The way most things are taught.

The fix wasn't to stop praising behavior. Praise matters. Feedback matters.

The fix was to add something that didn't depend on behavior at all: a declaration of unconditional identity.

"You are already loved. Not because of today. Just because you're you."

Said at bedtime. Said after hard moments. Said for no particular reason.

Children are building their internal model of how love works in these early years. And the model they build shows up later — in how they handle rejection, failure, success, relationships.

This work — planted early, repeated consistently — is worth doing.

For those of us building for children and families: this is the territory.

#parenthood #childdevelopment #earlychildhood #alreadyloved #parentingeducation

tweetThread.text:
My 4-year-old asked "are you proud of me?" after eating pasta.

Not excited. Just... checking.

And I realized I taught her to do that.

A thread on the one thing I changed 💛

Every "good job" after every meal. Every "I'm so proud" after every achievement.

All of it loving. All of it, over time, quietly teaching:

"Love is a response to performance."

I didn't mean to. But I did.

The fix isn't to stop praising behavior.

It's to separate praise from identity.

And add, at bedtime:

"You are already loved. Not because of today. Just because you're you."

Say it tonight. Mean it. Repeat it.

She still asks "are you proud of me?"

But now she asks it like she already knows the answer.

That's the goal.

→ Full story: alreadylovedkids.com/blog/teaching-kids-unconditional-love
